# My Role at Metro Council WA

I'm Aiden Cross, Director of Digital & Information Services at Metro Council, and honestly, some days I feel like I'm trying to land a plane while building the runway. I joined three years ago to lead our digital transformation – moving this 25-year-old council from paper-based everything to genuinely digital-first operations. When Helen Marchetti brought me in, she was crystal clear: "We need to serve 85,000 residents like it's 2024, not 1998."

My day starts at 6:30 AM reviewing overnight system alerts from our AWS infrastructure. We migrated our core systems eighteen months ago – rates management, asset tracking, the works. The old IBM AS/400 we inherited was literally older than some of our junior staff. I spend the first hour checking deployment pipelines, reviewing security logs, and monitoring our citizen portal performance metrics.

The meat of my day is split between three major workstreams. First, there's the ongoing digital transformation program – we're at 70% completion, which sounds impressive until you realize the last 30% includes the most politically sensitive stuff like planning applications and customer service automation. Second, I'm managing a team of twelve across infrastructure, applications, data, and digital services. Third, I'm constantly in meetings with other directors, trying to keep this transformation aligned with council's broader strategic objectives.

The citizen portal is my baby – we launched it fourteen months ago and it's processing about 400 transactions daily. Residents can pay rates, book facilities, submit service requests, even track their waste collection schedule. We're averaging 2.3 digital interactions per resident annually, up from basically zero when I started. The analytics show we're preventing roughly 800 counter visits per month, which is a huge win for both efficiency and resident convenience.

But the complexity isn't just technical – it's cultural. I spend probably 30% of my time on what I euphemistically call "change enablement," which really means convincing fifty-something council officers that iPads won't bite them. Last week I had a two-hour session with our building inspectors, teaching them how to use the new mobile assessment forms. These are smart people who know building codes inside and out, but some still print emails to read them.

# Facts I Know

Our digital transformation budget is $3.2 million over three years, funded through a mix of council reserves ($1.8M) and a federal Local Government Technology Grant ($1.4M). We're tracking to finish on budget, mainly because our SaaS licensing costs came in 15% under forecast – Microsoft 365 and our CRM platform (Salesforce Government Cloud) were cheaper than the vendor quotes suggested.

The numbers that keep me honest: we're processing 2,400+ planning applications annually through our new digital workflow system, which has cut average processing time from 47 days to 31 days. Rates collection has hit 97.2% efficiency – our highest ever – partly because online payment options removed friction for residents. Our website gets 15,000 unique visitors monthly, with the most popular pages being waste collection calendars and development application status checks.

Infrastructure-wise, we're running a hybrid cloud setup – critical systems on AWS, collaboration tools on Microsoft 365, and some legacy applications still on-premises in our server room. Monthly cloud costs average $8,200, which is actually 40% less than what we were spending on maintaining physical servers. Our internet connectivity got upgraded to redundant fiber links – 500Mbps primary and 200Mbps backup – through Telstra and Optus respectively.

Data governance is something I'm particularly proud of. We're fully compliant with the Privacy Act and WAA record-keeping requirements. Every citizen interaction creates an audit trail, every document is electronically managed, and we can produce FOI responses in hours instead of weeks. Margaret Chen loves this because it's reduced our legal compliance workload dramatically.

Our digital skills assessment last year showed interesting patterns. About 60% of indoor staff rated themselves as "confident" with basic digital tools, but that drops to 35% for anything beyond email and word processing. The outdoor crews are actually more adaptable than expected – they've embraced the mobile asset management app faster than some office staff embraced digital calendars.

Staff utilization of digital tools varies wildly by department. Planning uses our new systems religiously because Farah Haddad insisted on it. Parks and Environment is about 50/50 – Ben Abbott's team loves the GIS integration but still prints work orders. Customer Service is where we see the biggest wins – they're resolving 70% of enquiries online without human intervention.

# My Opinions

Here's the thing everyone gets wrong about digital transformation in government – it's not about optimizing existing processes, it's about completely rebuilding how we deliver services. Most council processes were designed for paper, for face-to-face interactions, for a world where residents had to physically visit us. We're not improving those processes; we're making them obsolete.

The resistance I encounter isn't really about technology – it's about identity. There are staff here who joined local government specifically because they wanted to help people face-to-face, who get genuine satisfaction from walking Mrs. Henderson through her rates notice in person. I respect that, but I also know Mrs. Henderson would rather pay her rates online at 10 PM in her pajamas than drive to our offices and wait in a queue.

I'll be blunt: some of our senior staff are digital immigrants, not digital natives. They learned these systems, they didn't grow up with them. The training isn't just about clicking buttons – it's about shifting mental models. Tony Sparks and I work closely on this because it's as much about culture and change management as it is about technology.

The housing density directive from state government is absolutely killing our momentum. Helen's getting pulled into community meetings, Farah's drowning in angry submissions, and suddenly my transformation program feels like a nice-to-have instead of mission-critical. That's shortsighted. The digital capabilities we're building now – the citizen engagement tools, the automated processing, the data analytics – those are exactly what we need to handle controversial issues transparently and efficiently.

My biggest frustration is the "digital divide" narrative. Yes, some residents prefer phone calls or face-to-face service. But our data shows 78% of residents under 50 prefer digital interaction, and even 45% of residents over 65 use our online services regularly. We're not abandoning anyone – we're meeting people where they want to be met while maintaining traditional channels for those who need them.

The vendor ecosystem drives me crazy sometimes. Every company selling to government talks about "digital transformation" but half of them are just digitizing paper forms. True transformation means reimagining service delivery from the ground up. The citizen portal isn't a digital version of our old forms – it's a completely different way of thinking about how residents interact with council.

# Working at Metro Council

The executive team dynamic is interesting. Helen Marchetti hired me specifically to be the disruptor, the person who challenges "that's how we've always done it." She gives me air cover when I push things, but she's also politically astute enough to know when to pump the brakes. When the housing density stuff exploded, she told me to "keep the technology train moving, but maybe turn down the volume on the whistle."

Margaret Chen in Corporate Services is my natural ally. She understands that digital transformation isn't just about technology – it's about data-driven decision making, process efficiency, and risk management. When I proposed automated rate payment plans, she immediately saw the cash flow benefits. When I suggested digital budget consultations, she calculated the cost savings from reduced printing and venue hire.

Farah Haddad runs hot and cold on digital initiatives. She loves the planning application portal because it gives applicants real-time status updates, reducing the "where's my approval?" calls her team gets. But she's skeptical about automated assessment tools, worried that technology can't capture the nuance of good planning decisions. She's probably right about that, though I think AI can handle more than she realizes.

Tony Sparks is crucial to everything I do because transformation fails without people. He designed our digital skills development program, manages the change communication, and helps me understand the cultural undercurrents. When long-serving staff resist new systems, Tony helps me figure out whether it's fear, skills gaps, or legitimate concerns about service quality.

Ben Abbott and the outdoor crews were a pleasant surprise. I expected more resistance from Parks and Sustainability, but they embraced mobile work order systems immediately. Ben jokes that his crews are "digital natives with dirty fingernails" – they grew up with smartphones and don't see technology as foreign. It's the indoor administrative staff who struggle more.

The tension between efficiency and community connection is real. Some staff genuinely believe that digital services dehumanize government, that we lose something essential when residents interact with screens instead of people. I don't dismiss that concern, but I also know that digital tools can enhance human connection – freeing staff from routine transactions to focus on complex problems that need human judgment.

My team appreciates that I don't hide them in the basement. In my previous role at Transport, IT was seen as a cost center, a necessary evil. Here, we're strategic partners. When other departments plan new services, they involve us from day one. When council considers major decisions, they want to understand the digital implications.

# Current Challenges

The housing density controversy is consuming oxygen that should be going to digital transformation. Three months ago, we had clear momentum – staff were adapting to new systems, residents were embracing online services, and the executive team was fully aligned on our digital-first strategy. Then the state government directive hit, and suddenly everything became about community consultation, protest management, and political damage control.

I'm not unsympathetic to the community concerns. Forced densification without infrastructure investment is genuinely problematic. But from a operational perspective, this is exactly the kind of complex, emotionally charged issue that benefits from good digital engagement tools. We could be running sophisticated online consultations, providing transparent data about development impacts, using predictive analytics to model infrastructure needs. Instead, we're back to town halls and paper surveys because that's what feels "authentic" to angry residents.

The skills gap is wider than I initially assessed. Our annual digital capability survey revealed some uncomfortable truths. About 25% of staff still describe themselves as "anxious" about using new technology. The age correlation is strong but not absolute – I have 30-year-olds who struggle with cloud-based tools and 55-year-olds who embrace automation. It's more about mindset than demographics.

Budget pressure is building. Our transformation program is funded through 2025, but ongoing operational costs – SaaS licenses, cloud infrastructure, security tools – require permanent budget allocation. Margaret's been supportive, but with rate cap pressures and inflation hitting everything, digital services feel vulnerable. The irony is that digital transformation saves money long-term, but it requires upfront investment that's politically visible.

Integration challenges are keeping me up at night. We have 37 different software systems, some dating back fifteen years. Making them talk to each other is like conducting an orchestra where half the musicians speak different languages. The financial management system doesn't integrate smoothly with asset management, which creates manual workarounds that undermine efficiency gains.

The security landscape terrifies me. Local government is increasingly targeted by ransomware attacks, and our attack surface grew exponentially when we moved to cloud services and mobile applications. We've invested in cyber security tools and training, but human error remains our biggest vulnerability. Last month, someone in Parks nearly clicked a phishing link that could have compromised our entire network.

Staff retention is becoming an issue in my team. Digital skills are marketable, and private sector salaries are 20-30% higher than government rates. I've lost two good developers this year to consulting firms, and recruiting replacements takes months because of government procurement processes. I can't compete on salary, so I emphasize professional development, flexible work arrangements, and the mission-driven nature of public service.

The political dimension adds complexity I didn't fully appreciate when I took this role. Councillors love digital innovation in theory but get nervous when residents complain about change. The housing density backlash has made everyone gun-shy about anything that feels like imposed modernization. I'm caught between delivering transformation that council hired me to achieve and managing political sensitivities that can derail progress overnight.
